spotlight: The Gulf war keeps redrawing the map: oil inventories are plunging, Europe braces for energy rationing, and Saudi Arabia and the UAE are now confirmed to have struck inside Iran while the Gulf monarchies press Trump to finish the job. At home, the budget law has unlocked tens of billions in transport contracts and a new retail bond emission, while ministers wrestle with gas storage and electricity relief for next winter.

stories.title: Government weighs cheaper electricity but no diesel tax cut

body.text: Deputy PM and industry minister Karel Havlíček (ANO) says he does not intend to lower the excise duty on diesel, but is considering relief in the regulated component of electricity prices

. In a separate interview, Havlíček elaborates on the search for a mechanism to secure enough gas for next winter, the question of filling storage facilities, the renationalisation of ČEZ and the American LNG contract

. The discussion comes against a backdrop of acute energy anxiety across the region, with Germany openly worried about the first energy rationing this summer as gas supplies remain unstable after the Gulf war
. The IEA, meanwhile, warns of further price spikes as global oil inventories drain at a record pace

stories.title: Transport contracts worth up to CZK 19bn start flowing after budget signed

body.text: Following the president's signature on the budget law, the Road and Motorway Directorate (ŘSD) and the Railway Administration are launching a wave of contract signings for transport projects worth up to 19 billion crowns

. The flurry of activity follows months of fiscal uncertainty that had held back infrastructure procurement. In parallel, finance minister Alena Schillerová has unveiled a new emission of Republic Bonds aimed at retail investors, again letting households lend their money to the state

. Separately, a state audit found that more than half of subsidies from the ministry for regional development earmarked for repairing municipal roads went to insignificant communications, raising questions about how transport money is allocated

stories.title: Czech municipalities invest again after 15 years of hoarding surpluses

body.text: For the first time in 15 years, Czech municipalities excluding Prague posted a deficit last year, as investment spending jumped by a quarter

. The shift away from years of accumulating surpluses is driven largely by the approaching municipal elections, which traditionally push local governments to spend on visible projects. The renewed appetite for investment marks a notable change in fiscal behaviour at the local level after a long period of caution. The trend also dovetails with the broader pickup in infrastructure spending now that national transport contracts are being signed in volume.

stories.title: Italian U-Power buys Czech Cerva Group to create CZK 12bn workwear firm

body.text: Italian workwear maker U-Power has completed its acquisition of the Czech company Cerva Group, creating a combined business with revenue exceeding 12 billion crowns

. The deal consolidates two established players in the protective and work clothing market and gives the Italian buyer a substantial Central European footprint. In a separate cross-border move highlighting Czech investors' continued appetite abroad, Czech investors have closed another billion-crown deal in Poland, acquiring an established shopping centre

stories.title: Indebted Solek presents new reorganisation plan with lower payout

body.text: The solar group Solek has presented an updated reorganisation plan offering lower repayment to creditors, after its assets were revalued at just 83 million crowns

. The sharply reduced valuation underscores the depth of the company's financial troubles and leaves creditors facing a smaller recovery than previously expected. The revised plan is a key test of whether the heavily indebted group can be restructured rather than wound up.

stories.title: Honda posts first-ever annual loss after retreating from EVs

body.text: Honda has reported the first annual loss in its history after pulling back from investment in electric vehicles

. The result is a stark sign of how badly the strategic retreat has hit the Japanese carmaker's finances. The EV pullback theme has a long arc: a separate retrospective notes that electric cars were once a big deal at the turn of the century — the last century — a reminder that the technology's fortunes have swung repeatedly over more than a hundred years

stories.title: German layoff wave hits VW, BioNTech and others

body.text: Volkswagen, BioNTech and other German companies are announcing a wave of layoffs, and analysts fear it is only the beginning

. The job cuts point to mounting pressure on Germany's industrial core amid high energy costs and structural challenges. The strain is acute in chemicals, where the Gulf conflict, elevated energy prices and Chinese competition are pushing the European chemicals industry to breaking point

stories.title: Xi warns Trump that Taiwan is the make-or-break issue at Beijing summit

body.text: Xi Jinping warned Trump at the opening of their Beijing summit that the Taiwan question is the critical point in US-China relations
. Trump's stated intention to discuss US arms sales to Taiwan with Xi has rattled Asian allies, who fear Washington could trade away their security
. The NYT, in an analytical read, contrasts the two leaders' demeanour — Trump was flattering, Xi was resolute, and the difference spoke volumes about who set the terms

stories.title: Saudis and UAE confirmed to have struck inside Iran as monarchies press Trump

body.text: US officials say Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates carried out secret attacks inside Iran
. The Gulf monarchies — Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Bahrain and Qatar — now want Trump to ensure the US-Israeli operation ends with Iran's military capabilities destroyed, since the war has already begun
. At the same time, Riyadh is hedging diplomatically: the FT reports Saudi Arabia is floating a non-aggression pact with Tehran in response to the American-Israeli military operation
. Lebanon and Israel, meanwhile, are heading to Washington for a third round of direct talks on extending their ceasefire as it nears its end

stories.title: US abruptly cancels deployment of 4,000 troops to Poland

body.text: The Pentagon has cancelled the planned deployment of 4,000 American troops to Poland, a move that has stirred unease across Europe
. The reversal comes at a sensitive moment for the alliance, with all eyes on Marco Rubio at a NATO foreign ministers' meeting in Sweden, where the US position within the alliance is in focus
. The uncertainty over American commitment lands as both Russia and Ukraine remain cool on US-led peace talks even after the end of the Middle East war

stories.title: Experts warn of Russian spring offensive as Ukraine builds its own defences

body.text: Analysts warn that once nature turns green, the Russians will start attacking again, while Ukraine is meanwhile sending hundreds of drones daily deep into Russian targets, including a strike on the Primorsk oil terminal
. To reduce its reliance on the United States, Ukrainian arms maker Fire Point is launching its own satellites and aims to cut Kyiv's dependence on American technology
. The push for technological self-sufficiency reflects deepening doubts in Kyiv about the durability of US support as Washington reverses troop deployments in the region.

stories.title: Higher education reform to weaken academic senates, strengthen rectors

body.text: The education minister has presented a reform of higher education that would weaken academic senates, strengthen the powers of rectors and create a new university council

. The proposal marks a significant shift in university governance toward more centralised, executive-style leadership. Separately, new education minister Plaga (ANO) has admitted that the radical plan to slash the number of secondary school fields did not work out, and the reform is being postponed by a year

stories.title: Court throws out 25-year Teslamp lawsuit against Česká spořitelna

body.text: A court has dismissed the 25-year-old lawsuit brought by the insolvency administrator of Teslamp against Česká spořitelna
. The administrator had sought four billion crowns from the bank; instead, he must now pay over 100 million crowns toward the bank's legal costs
. The outcome brings a dramatic end to one of the longest-running commercial disputes in Czech courts, turning a multibillion claim into a heavy liability for the plaintiff side.

stories.title: Greens take Munich mayoralty for the first time, ending 42 years of SPD rule

body.text: Thirty-five-year-old Dominik Krause of the Greens has defeated Social Democrat Reiter to become mayor of Munich
. His victory ends 42 years of uninterrupted SPD rule in the Bavarian capital and marks the first time a Green politician has led the city
. The result is a notable symbolic shift in one of Germany's largest and most prosperous cities, traditionally a Social Democratic stronghold.

stories.title: Bank results once again a source of stability in turbulent times
stories.author: Karel Nedvěd

body.text: According to Fio banka analyst Karel Nedvěd, the solid full-year results of Erste, Komerční banka and Moneta for the fourth quarter of 2025 did not disappoint

. He frames the banking sector's performance as a point of certainty in an otherwise turbulent period, with strong earnings underpinning confidence in the lenders. The commentary suggests that, amid geopolitical and economic uncertainty, the banks' resilient profitability offers investors a rare anchor.

stories.title: A return to the past — does another inflation spike await?
stories.author: Julie Hrstková

body.text: Julie Hrstková warns of the risks of a renewed inflation spike in connection with rising oil prices after the Gulf war

. She argues the dynamics evoke earlier inflationary episodes, with energy costs once again threatening to feed through into broader prices. The piece cautions that policymakers and households alike may face a return to conditions they had hoped were behind them.

stories.title: Czech economy grew three times faster than Slovakia's — and the gap will widen
stories.author: Anna Šuhajdová

body.text: Anna Šuhajdová compares the economic growth of the Czech Republic at 2.6 percent with Slovakia's 0.8 percent, arguing the divergence will deepen this year as well

. She sees structural factors driving the widening gap rather than a one-off, suggesting the two neighbouring economies are on increasingly different trajectories. The commentary frames the comparison as a warning sign for Slovakia's longer-term competitiveness.

stories.title: Why Prague has stopped building offices
stories.author: Pavel Kadera

body.text: Pavel Kadera explains that Prague's office market is experiencing a slowdown in new construction, driven by two trends

. Developers are increasingly drawn to residential projects, while new office buildings now pay off only in central locations. The shift reflects changing demand and economics in the capital's commercial property sector, with the office model squeezed outside prime areas.

stories.title: Investment bubbles are a normal feature of financial markets
stories.author: Michal Stupavský

body.text: Conseq investment strategist Michal Stupavský discusses theories of investment bubbles according to Grantham, Shiller, Soros and Buffett, emphasising the return of valuations to their long-term average

. He treats bubbles as a recurring, almost inherent characteristic of markets rather than an aberration. The commentary lands amid global enthusiasm for AI stocks, where investors are chasing the few European plays such as STMicroelectronics, Aixtron and Nokia as the US tech frenzy goes global

stories.title: Are fears over corporate debt and private credit justified?
stories.author: Jiří Król

body.text: Jiří Król argues that the debate over the valuation of private credit portfolios and the impact of AI on the software sector is exaggerated

. He pushes back against the prevailing alarm about the quality of corporate debt, suggesting concerns are overstated. The German perspective is more cautious: a FAZ analysis of the private credit market points to rising fund withdrawals, valuation questions and worries about AI's effect on software

stories.title: How Britain found itself in a debt cage

body.text: A deep FT Big Read examines how Britain ended up trapped in a structure of high public debt after decades of neglected reforms

. The analysis traces the long accumulation of fiscal constraints that now limit the government's room for manoeuvre. It argues that successive failures to reform have left the country boxed in, with debt servicing increasingly shaping policy choices.
